summary: Every effective ad follows a narrative arc — a structured emotional progression from the viewer's current state to the desired outcome. Arc selection determines beat count, ratio, and the emotional logic of every scene.
principles: Six canonical ad arcs: problem-solution (frustration → resolution), desire-fulfillment (aspiration → achievement), contrast (before → after), journey (departure → transformation → arrival), social-proof (skepticism → evidence → trust), demo-reveal (curiosity → demonstration → payoff).

Each arc has a beat ratio table that determines time allocation: problem-solution gives 25% to the problem and 25% to the proof; desire-fulfillment gives 25% to desire painting and 30% to fulfillment.
Arc selection is driven by product category + audience + platform: tech products favor demo-reveal; lifestyle brands favor desire-fulfillment; challenger brands favor contrast.
The hook must establish the arc type in the first 2 seconds so the viewer knows the emotional contract: is this a journey or a demonstration?
Every beat must advance the arc — a scene that stalls or repeats the same emotional register is wasted time.
The CTA must complete the arc's promise: the call to action is the viewer's next step in the story, not an interruption of it.
Luxury and prestige brands can break arc conventions with slow-burn or abstract structures, but the break must be intentional and documented.
Arc-to-script mapping ensures each beat produces a required script section with defined emotional target, intensity, and visual constraint.
apply_when: The ad has a duration longer than 6 seconds and multiple scenes.
The brief describes a clear viewer transformation or journey.
The production needs structural guidance for scene planning and pacing.
avoid_when: The ad is a single shot or static image with no narrative progression.
The brand style guide mandates a specific non-arc structure.
The platform format (e.g., bumper ads) constrains to a single moment with no arc.
failure_patterns: No arc at all: a sequence of features or shots with no emotional progression — the viewer watches but doesn't feel anything.
Wrong arc for the product: desire-fulfillment for a cleaning product (nobody aspires to clean) instead of problem-solution.
Arc that doesn't resolve: the story builds tension but the CTA doesn't pay it off, leaving the viewer emotionally stranded.
Too many arcs in one ad: trying to be a journey AND a demo AND a contrast in 30 seconds creates structural chaos.

cross_domain_notes.note: The arc determines the intensity curve shape: problem-solution escalates, desire-fulfillment builds gradually, contrast alternates, social-proof fluctuates.
The single promise determines which arc to use: promise of transformation → journey, promise of proof → social-proof, promise of upgrade → contrast.
The audience's self-image determines which arc resonates: identity-driven audiences prefer journeys, practical audiences prefer demos and proof.

execution_techniques: Map the arc to concrete beat durations before writing: for a 30-second problem-solution, that's roughly 4s hook + 7s problem + 5s solution intro + 7s proof + 4s resolution + 3s CTA.
Write the hook to signal the arc type immediately: 'Tired of X?' signals problem-solution; 'Imagine if...' signals desire-fulfillment; side-by-side signals contrast.
End each beat on a forward-looking moment that pulls the viewer into the next beat rather than closing each scene neatly.
Test the CTA against the arc promise: if the arc is 'transformation through X,' the CTA must offer the viewer their own transformation, not just a purchase link.
